title: Suspecting AI cheating, Ivy League prof ordered an in-person final; scores fell 50%
gist: A Brown University economics professor ran a natural experiment showing take-home exam averages of 96 collapsed to 48 on an in-person final, with 27 students dropping or skipping, exposing the scale of AI-assisted cheating among elite students and raising questions about whether AI use is substituting for real learning.

insights.claim: In a Brown University ECON 1170 section, take-home midterm scores averaged 96 out of 100 with 40 students scoring a perfect 100, versus a historical midterm average of 65 to 80 percent for a harder-than-usual exam.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: Quantifies how dramatically AI inflates take-home assessment scores, a signal for the assessment-integrity and proctoring software market.
insights.quote: The results of the midterm exam, which was administered on March 5, were extraordinary, with an average score of 96 out of 100. Forty students scored a perfect 100.
insights.locator: The course… typically attracts few students

insights.claim: When the professor switched to an in-person final, the average score plunged from 96 to 48, a roughly 50 percent drop.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: The gap between take-home and proctored scores is a direct measure of AI substitution for learning, relevant to edtech and credential-value debates.
insights.quote: Among those who took the test, the average score plunged—from 96 all the way down to 48.
insights.locator: Among those who took the test

insights.claim: After the professor announced an in-person final, 18 students dropped the course and 9 skipped the final, and 22 of those 27 had scored a perfect 100 on the take-home midterm.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: The self-selection of top scorers out of the proctored exam is strong circumstantial evidence of systematic cheating.
insights.quote: Eighteen students suddenly dropped the course, while nine others didn't even attend the final exam. Of those 27 students… 22 had scored a perfect 100 in the midterm exam.

insights.claim: Course enrollment jumped from a historical maximum of about 30 (sometimes as few as 8) to 86 students, likely drawn by the new take-home evaluation system.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: Assessment format itself drives student behavior and course selection, an incentive-design lesson for institutions.

insights.claim: Even correct exam answers felt slightly off with a 'very convoluted style,' and running the questions through ChatGPT produced similar results, suggesting AI authorship.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: Stylistic fingerprinting of AI output is becoming an informal detection method faculty rely on absent reliable tools.
insights.quote: Beyond the numbers, many of the answers, even when correct, felt slightly off. They had a "very convoluted style,"

insights.claim: A recent survey of Princeton students found 29.9 percent admitted to cheating with AI on at least one exam or assignment.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: Baseline prevalence figure sizing the AI-academic-integrity problem across elite universities.
insights.quote: A recent survey of Princeton students found that 29.9 percent admitted to cheating with AI on at least one exam or assignment.

insights.claim: A Brown provost-led report found 56 percent of undergraduates and 67 percent of graduate/medical students use generative AI daily or weekly, yet large majorities also fear negative consequences for their cognitive capacity.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: High adoption paired with self-reported cognitive fear signals demand for AI tools that augment rather than replace thinking.
insights.quote: 56 percent of undergraduate respondents [at Brown] and 67 percent of graduate and medical student respondents reported intentionally using GenAI tools daily or weekly
insights.locator: As a university, Brown is grappling

insights.claim: The professor frames widespread AI cheating as an existential societal risk, arguing a society where a significant fraction of top young minds accept cheating leads to decline.
insights.speaker: Nate Anderson
insights.so_what: Captures the elite-institutional backlash narrative that may drive policy and a return to proctored, in-person assessment.
insights.quote: We cannot afford to have a society in which a significant fraction of our best young minds think that cheating is okay… That leads to a declining society, to a failed society.
